data.title: Press release
data.description:
Custos Maritime & Defence Industries AB

Shareholders are entilted to subscribe for ten (10) new shares at a price of SEK 0.005 per share for each warrant

Directed Share Issues and Warrants Registered with the Swedish Companies Registration Office

 

Custos Maritime & Defence Industries AB (publ) (formerly RightBridge Ventures Group AB (publ)) announces that the directed share issues and the issuance of warrants resolved by the Extraordinary General Meeting on 9 September 2025 have now been registered with the Swedish Companies Registration Office (Bolagsverket).

The new warrants have been registered as series TO3 2025/2025, as the Company already has two previous warrant series (TO1 and TO2) in Euroclear. Each warrant entitles the holder to subscribe for ten (10) new shares at a price of SEK 0.005 per share.

The Board will extend the subscription period for the exercise of the options to provide all shareholders with sufficient time to exercise them. The final deadline will be announced later.

 

Key Dates for Distribution of Warrants (TO3 2025/2025)

 

15 October 2025:  Last day of trading including the right to receive TO3

16 October 2025:  First day of trading excluding the right to receive TO3

17 October 2025:  Record date for distribution

21 October 2025:  TO3 warrants are booked to respective VP accounts/custody accounts

Stockholm, 10th october 2025

Per Olav Karlsen

CEO / Boardmember

+47 41 78 50 50

pok@cleaves.no

 

https://news.cision.com/custos-maritime---defence-industries-ab/r/press-release%2Cc4248624

data.title: Pinnacle West Capital Is Maintained at Neutral by UBS
Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1118 ET - Shares in European semiconductor companies are in decline after President Trump said he was considering a "massive increase" of tariffs and "many other countermeasures" on Chinese products coming into the U.S. Shares of Dutch chip-making equipment supplier ASML Holding and smaller rival ASM International are down more than 2%. Shares of BE Semiconductor Industries, the Dutch supplier of semiconductor assembly equipment, are down 3%. German chip maker Infineon Technologies logs a 2.6% decline. Shares of STMicroelectronics, which supplies to clients like Elon Musk's Tesla and Apple, fall 1.8%. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0921 ET - Travel by Canadians to the U.S. shows no sign of recovering as tensions between the country and its nearest neighbor remain high. Canadian-resident return trips from overseas increased 3.9% on-year in September, but air travel to the U.S. slumped 27.1%, Statistics Canada data shows. Return trips by automobile to the U.S. were down a ninth straight month, falling a steep 34.8%. The same month, non-resident trips to Canada by air rose a modest 1.9% on-year, though arrivals by U.S. residents slipped 0.4%. U.S.-resident trips to Canada by automobile declined 5.3%, an eight consecutive drop. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0852 ET - Retailers have slowed down on raising prices to offset tariffs, say analysts Brian Nagel and Andrew Chasanoff, citing an Oppenheimer analysis of 200 likely tariff-impacted items. As of Oct. 6, prices on 65 of the items were up compared with April 28 by an average of 17%. That is consistent with a previous reading in early September, suggesting price increases may be slowing down. Companies are still focused on protecting margins, but in the near-term, they may be wary of underlying consumer demand ahead of the holiday selling season and also may be waiting out potential court action on tariff policy, the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0739 ET - U.S. tariffs are making some Europeans more concerned about their jobs, European Central Bank economists say in a blog post. About 15% of workers say they have a higher probability of job loss, with 3% saying that the chance of losing their jobs had increased a lot, according to a July ECB survey. The share of workers more afraid of job loss is higher in countries where the workforce is more exposed to U.S. exports, such as Ireland, the economists say. Workers in more trade-oriented and cyclical sectors, which are currently under pressure from other shocks like high energy prices, are also likely to believe their jobs are at risk. However, 85% of all workers shrug off concerns, reporting unchanged or even lower job-loss expectations, the economists add. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0633 ET - U.S. and European government bond yields fall, as the U.S. government shutdown continues, while in Europe investors wait for French President Emmanuel Macron's expected announcement of a new prime minister later in the day. "In the U.S., the government shutdown continued, delaying key economic data and complicating the Federal Reserve's policy outlook," XTB MENA's Milad Azar says in a note. The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield falls 3.9 basis points to 4.108%; the 10-year German Bund yield is down 2.8bps to 2.677%; and the 10-year French OAT yield falls 3.7bps to 3.490%, according to Tradeweb data. U.K. gilt and Scandinavian government bond yields also trade lower. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0628 ET - China's expanded export controls on rare earths appear aimed at boosting its leverage in U.S. trade negotiations, Capital Economics says. President Trump and Chinese leader Xi Jinping are due to meet on the sidelines of an APEC summit in South Korea in a few weeks' time, economist Julian Evans-Pritchard says. "While the timing of the [rare earths] move may be opportunistic, we suspect the new controls are mostly motivated by medium-term geostrategic goals," CE says. China's stranglehold on rare earths won't last forever, as deposits outside the country are plentiful. But Beijing could shore up the position of its manufacturing sector and hold back foreign competitors before they manage to reduce reliance on Chinese supply, Evans-Pritchard says. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0410 ET - In government bond markets the vigilantes are restless, says AXA Investment Managers' Chris Iggo in a note. "There is a general discontent with how public finances are being managed," the CIO of AXA IM Core says. Populism and the associated political fragmentation tend to work against governments taking hard decisions on taxes and spending, says Iggo, who is also Chair of the AXA IM Investment Institute. "Note the current situation in France and the U.K.," he says. There is a bit of a stalemate in the bond markets with long yields stabilizing as investors wait for budget news in the U.K. and France and being placated in the U.S. by the prospect of lower Federal Reserve rates and ongoing growth, Iggo says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0400 ET - Gold prices slip back beneath the $4,000 mark, but remain on track for an eighth consecutive weekly gain as structural drivers stay in place. Futures in New York rise 0.2% to $3,982.10 a troy ounce in early trade and are up 1.9% on week. The retreat from record highs reflects profit-taking pressures and easing geopolitical risks after Israel signed off on President Trump's plan for a cease-fire and hostage release in Gaza. However, any pullback is expected to be short-lived. The Federal Reserve is still expected to cut interest rates further, supporting non-yielding gold, while the prolonged U.S. government shutdown is injecting new uncertainty into markets. "If the shutdown does persist into next week, we won't get the CPI release on Wednesday, which will leave us increasingly uncertain about the U.S. economy," analysts at Deutsche Bank say. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0341 ET - Oil prices fall in early trade after Israel approved the U.S.-brokered deal for a cease-fire and hostage release in Gaza. "This presents a major step toward ending the two-year war that raised the risk of supply disruptions in the oil market," analysts at ANZ Research say. Brent crude and WTI are both down 0.5% to $64.88 and $60.76 a barrel, respectively, after settling 1.6% lower in the previous session. Prices are also pressured by concerns over an impending supply glut as OPEC+ continues to boost production and the latest U.S. crude stockpiles data point to weaker demand. However, further losses are limited by new U.S. sanctions on Iran, with the Treasury Department targeting more than 50 individuals, entities and vessels facilitating Iranian oil and liquefied petroleum gas sales and shipments. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0313 ET - Bitcoin edges higher as the prolonged U.S. government shutdown and political uncertainty elsewhere drives investors toward alternative assets. The U.S. government shutdown is well into its second week and has started to disrupt businesses, with some companies facing payment delays and considering layoffs. In France, President Emmanuel Macron is expected to name a new prime minister Friday to replace Sebastien Lecornu after he resigned less than a month into the role, although uncertainty remains elevated. Bitcoin rises 0.1% to $121,339, having reached a record high of $126,223 Monday, LSEG data show. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0248 ET - The dollar's recent recovery stalls, with the currency falling slightly after reaching a two-month high in the previous session. The dollar has strengthened this week as political concerns in Japan and France weighed on the yen and the euro. Sanae Takaichi's victory in the leadership election of Japan's ruling party prompted markets to bet on lower-for-longer interest rates. However, the yen received some reprieve after Takaichi said in an interview on Japanese TV that she has no intention of triggering an excessively weak yen. In France, President Emmanuel Macron is expected to name a new prime minister Friday, easing concerns that snap elections will be called. The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 99.351 after reaching a high of 99.563 Thursday. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

2347 ET - The U.S.-China tariff truce, while fragile, could continue, as a hard trade decoupling has proved undesirable for both sides, economists at Citi Research say in a note. Both countries could be strengthening their leverage ahead of a potential meeting on trade between their presidents, they say. China tightened rare-earth export controls further and added more companies to the "unreliable entity list." The U.S. is set to impose port fees on Chinese-built, owned and operated ships this month, and U.S. lawmakers have called for broader bans on chip-making equipment. Export controls, China's purchase of U.S. goods and potential investment plans, China's opening up--especially of the services sector--and fentanyl tariffs could all figure at the possible summit, they add. (monica.gupta@wsj.com)

data.description: Shares in European semiconductor companies are in decline after President Trump said he was considering a "massive increase" of tariffs and "many other countermeasures" on Chinese products coming into the U.S. Shares of Dutch chip-making equipment supplier ASML Holding and smaller rival ASM International are down more than 2%. data.title: PennyMac Fincl Services Is Maintained at Neutral by UBS
Carney Drops More Hints About Austerity in Budget Plan — Market Talk
data.description: Canada PM Mark Carney drops more hints that spending cuts will be part of the Liberal government's annual budget plan to be unveiled Nov. 4. While unveiling some family-oriented tax measures at an event in Ottawa, he says the budget plan will contain "pragmatic and responsible" decisions about expenditures. He has argued about spending cuts in the bureaucracy to free up cash to help kickstart the kind of infrastructure and resource projects that Canada needs to reboot its economy, and protect program that assist the most vulnerable households. Carney has been critical of spending decisions from his Liberal predecessor, Justin Trudeau. Economists say the annual budget deficit for the current fiscal year will exceed 2% of GDP, and perhaps reach close to 3%. (paul.vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

data.title: Trump says weighing 'massive increase' in tariffs on Chinese imports, no reason to meet with Xi
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump on Friday said there no reason to meet with China's President Xi Jinping in two weeks in South Korea as planned, adding in a Truth Social post that the U.S. is calculating a massive increase in tariffs on Chinese imports.

Trump said China has been sending letters to countries worldwide saying it planned to impose export controls on every element of production related to rare earths.

"Nobody has ever seen anything like this but, essentially, it would 'clog' the Markets, and make life difficult for virtually every Country in the World, especially for China", he said the Truth Social post.

He added that he had not spoken to China's Xi Jinping because there was no reason to do so.

data.title: Taptravel Nordic AB (publ) receives observation status
data.description:
Nasdaq Stockholm ABBond Market information

Taptravel Nordic AB (publ) receives observation status

On October 7, 2025, Taptravel Nordic AB (publ) (the “Company”) disclosed a

press release with information that one of the Company's lenders has invoked a

payment block. As long as the payment block is in place, the Company may not

make any payments under its bonds, including interest payments.

The rules of Nasdaq Stockholm state that an issuer's financial instruments may

be given observation status if there is substantial uncertainty in respect of

the issuer's financial position.

With reference to the above, Nasdaq Stockholm AB decides that the bond loan

issued by Taptravel Nordic AB (publ) (TAP2427, ISIN code NO0013326041) shall be

given observation status.

For further information about this exchange notice please contact Enforcement &

Investigations, telephone +46 8 405 70 50.https://view.news.eu.nasdaq.com/view?id=b8c6a9f01c7e2e0ccc3f45e477643027c&lang=en&src=omxlink

data.title: CAMS announces 1:5 stock split to enhance share liquidity and retail participation
data.description:
Registrar and transfer agent of mutual funds, Computer Age Management Services Ltd (CAMS), on Friday (October 10), said its board of directors has approved a stock split in the ratio of 1:5, sub-dividing one equity share of face value ₹10 each into five equity shares of ₹2 each, fully paid-up. The move is subject to shareholder approval through a postal ballot.

CAMS said the decision aims to enhance the liquidity of its equity shares, improve affordability and accessibility for retail investors, and broaden the shareholder base. Following the sub-division, the authorised share capital will remain unchanged at ₹51.25 crore, though the number of authorised shares will increase from 5,12,50,000 shares of ₹10 each to 25,62,50,000 shares of ₹2 each.

Similarly, the subscribed and paid-up share capital will remain at ₹49.53 crore, with the total number of equity shares rising from 4,95,30,127 shares of ₹10 each to 24,76,50,635 shares of ₹2 each.

The company added that the record date for the share split will be announced after receiving shareholder approval. The process is expected to be completed within two months from the date of such approval, subject to any statutory or regulatory clearances.

CAMS will also make a corresponding alteration to the Capital Clause of its Memorandum of Association to reflect the revised face value, pending shareholder consent. The company confirmed that it has issued only one class of equity shares, and hence, the sub-division applies uniformly to all shareholders.

Shares of Computer Age Management Services Ltd ended at ₹3,865.00, up by ₹33.90, or 0.88%, on the BSE.

data.title: Protagonist Has Market Value Over $4B — WSJ
Johnson & Johnson in Talks to Buy Protagonist Therapeutics — WSJ
data.description:
By Lauren Thomas and Ben Dummett

Johnson & Johnson is in talks to buy Protagonist Therapeutics in a deal that would solidify the companies' existing partnership, according to people familiar with the matter.

The details

A deal is not guaranteed and the exact details being discussed couldn't be learned, the people said.

Protagonist had a market value of over $4 billion as of Thursday's close. Including a typical premium, a deal would likely value the company well above that.

J&J is already working with Protagonist to develop an oral treatment for immune diseases including plaque psoriasis and ulcerative colitis and has the exclusive rights to commercialize the product. It already owns close to 4% of Protagonist's shares, according to FactSet.

By acquiring Protagonist, the healthcare conglomerate would also gain access to the drug rusfertide, from Protagonist and partner Takeda Pharmaceutical. Rusfertide has shown promise in late-stage testing in treating a rare blood cancer called polycythemia vera.

Both assets would complement J&J's portfolio of immune and cancer drugs.

Protagonist's stock is up over 70% so far this year through Thursday, fueled in part by the promising trial results of its treatments for plaque psoriasis — an autoimmune disease — and the bowel disease ulcerative colitis. Shares have gained more than 450% since the company's initial public offering in 2016.

Still, the deal would be easily digestible for J&J, which is worth close to $460 billion.

The context

J&J's immune-disease drug Stelara, one of the company's biggest sellers in recent years, began facing heavy competition from lower-cost copycats this year, and the company warned to expect accelerating sales losses.

The company has a number of products it is counting on to offset the losses. But like all drugmakers regularly dealing with patent expirations, J&J has turned to a mix of in-house development and acquisitions to refill its offerings.

In January, J&J struck a $15 billion deal to acquire mental-illness drug developer Intra-Cellular Therapies. More recently, Novo Nordisk agreed to acquire Akero Therapeutics for up to $5.2 billion, in a bid to gain access to the company's liver disease drug.

data.title: Johnson & Johnson in Talks to Acquire Protagonist Therapeutics, Sources Say — WSJ
J&J, Protagonist Have Existing Ties — WSJ
Tesla prices standard Model Y at $41,700 in Norway, its website shows
data.description:
Tesla has priced the new "affordable" version of its best-selling standard Model Y SUV at 421,996 Norwegian crowns ($41,714) in Norway, the electric vehicle maker's website showed on Friday.

In Germany, the model is priced at 39,990 euros ($46,304), according to Tesla's website. In Sweden, it is priced at 499,990 Swedish crowns ($52,521).

Tesla said the model would be available in Norway and Sweden in November or December.

Unveiled on Tuesday, the new version of the best-selling Model Y SUV will join a European market already crowded with budget EVs, with European and Chinese brands offering more than a dozen models below $30,000, and more to come.

($1 = 10.1163 Norwegian crowns)

($1 = 9.5198 Swedish crowns)

($1 = 0.8636 euros)

data.title: Wall Street falls after Trump warns of higher tariffs on China
data.description:
Wall Street's main indexes fell on Friday, erasing earlier gains, after U.S. President Donald Trump said he was weighing a "massive increase" in tariffs on Chinese goods.

At 11:12 a.m. ET, the Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 201.88 points, or 0.44%, to 46,156.54. The S&P 500 lost 43.06 points, or 0.64%, to 6,692.05 and the Nasdaq Composite lost 223.54 points, or 0.97%, to 22,801.09.

data.title: Canada, U.S. Have Discussed Reviving Keystone XL, PM Carney Says — Update
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA — Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney said he and President Trump have discussed the development of energy projects that would help both countries, including the twice-rejected Keystone XL expansion project.

Carney, who met with Trump this week in Washington, said the two leaders have talked about opportunities for cooperation between their countries. "We have opportunities in the energy sector. There are many possible projects, including Keystone XL, but there are others," he said. He added senior Canadian and U.S. officials are now in intense talks about which energy projects might be worth pursuing.

The White House spokesperson was not immediately available for comment. Carney said officials have spoken with private-sector investors about the possibility of a Keystone XL revival, as well as other resource projects. He was not privy to those talks, he added.

The expansion of the Keystone XL project has been a source of friction between the U.S. and Canada, when former presidents Barack Obama and Joe Biden were in power. Obama denied TC Energy a presidential permit. Biden, shortly after taking office, revoked a permit that Trump has issued for the Keystone XL expansion to proceed.

The project would link to existing pipelines and deliver oil from western Canada to Nebraska. It has faced setbacks amid opposition from landowners and environmentalists. Earlier this year, Trump signaled his interest in having the pipeline built.

South Bow, which was spun off from TC Energy last year, is the Keystone pipeline owner, and earlier this year the company said it had moved on from the project. A company spokeswoman didn't immediately return Friday a request for comment. Earlier this week, amid local media reports that Carney and Trump have discussed the revival of Keystone XL at the Oval Office, South Bow said it supported efforts to increase the transportation of Canadian crude oil to foreign markets.

Talk of reviving the Keystone XL expansion emerges as Carney is pushing for the construction of so-called nation-building efforts, like infrastructure, trade corridors and resource projects, to help rewire an economy trying to reduce its reliance on a protectionist U.S. government. About one-fifth of Canada's economic output is tied to trade with the U.S., and has left the country after being exposed to Trump's hefty tariff policy.

Carney's discussions with Trump on energy projects are also aimed at securing tariff relief on some key sectors, such as steel and aluminum, officials say. Canada is the biggest foreign supplier of both those metals to the U.S., and Canadian-made steel and aluminum face 50% tariffs. Besides energy, U.S. and Canadian officials are in talks about a deal to ease the tariff burden on Canadian steel and aluminum, Carney said.

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

data.title: MHIET and MHI-TC Complete Delivery of First "COORDY" Controller Providing Optimized Control of Multiple Power Sources
data.description:
TOKYO, Oct 10, 2025 - (JCN Newswire) - Mitsubishi Heavy Industries Engine & Turbocharger, Ltd. (MHIET) and MHI Transportation and Construction Engineering, Ltd. (MHI-TC), which are both Mitsubishi Heavy Industries (MHI) Group companies, have delivered the first "COORDY" microgrid controller enabling optimal control of multiple power sources and maximum use of renewable energy. Delivery was made to Mitsui Fudosan Logistics Park Yokohama-Shinkoyasu (MFLP Yokohama-Shinkoyasu), a large-scale logistics facility developed in Tsurumi-ku, Yokohama by Mitsui Fudosan Co., Ltd. and ENEOS Real Estate Corporation.

MFLP Yokohama-Shinkoyasu has a rooftop installation of a 2,000kW-class solar power generating facility and on-site storage batteries of roughly 2,600kWh to utilize the renewable energy. There, MHIET's COORDY provides optimized control of three power sources; grid power, the solar power system and energy storage system (batteries). With the use of COORDY, it is expected to reduce CO2 emissions by some 40% compared to using grid power for the demand. MHI-TC was in charge of all aspects in purchasing, installation and trial operation of COORDY as well as construction of civil engineering structures. MHI Group's total engineering capability(*), achieved through close coordination among all Group companies, has made it possible to deliver a total package of optimal operational facilities and products with comprehensive handling of everything from civil engineering to installations of equipment, resulting in outstanding construction efficiency and total cost reductions.

EBLOX is a "Triple Hybrid" stand-alone power supply system developed by MHIET that coordinates energy from three sources: renewable energy such as solar power, a reciprocating engine generator and storage batteries. This combination allows for optimal use and stabilized control of renewable energy, which is inherently vulnerable to fluctuations. Since 2019, this system has been in operation as a demonstration facility at MHIET's Sagamihara Plant. COORDY, which plays the key role in EBLOX, enables response to business continuity planning (BCP) needs and provides optimized control of power from the engine generator, storage batteries and renewable energy when grid energy is unavailable due to natural disasters, etc.

By proposing EBLOX as a solution that optimally utilize volatile renewable energy for stable power supply by way of COORDY, MHIET aims to achieve low-carbon/zero-carbon society along with MHI Group's strategic approach towards energy transition and contribute to solving diverse social challenges.

MHI-TC, through its comprehensive capabilities in civil engineering, facility construction, and plant construction and engineering, will continue to strive to contribute to an optimized and efficient sustainable society through utilization of MHI Group products and facilities.

(*) MHI Group's total engineering capabilities are described in detail in the following press release: https://www.mhi.co.jp/technology/review/pdf/e612/e612120.pdf

About MHI Group

Mitsubishi Heavy Industries (MHI) Group is one of the world's leading industrial groups, spanning energy, smart infrastructure, industrial machinery, aerospace and defense. MHI Group combines cutting-edge technology with deep experience to deliver innovative, integrated solutions that help to realize a carbon neutral world, improve the quality of life and ensure a safer world. For more information, please visit www.mhi.com or follow our insights and stories on spectra.mhi.com

Source: Mitsubishi Heavy Industries, Ltd.
